source.title: AI News: GPT-5.6 and the new Super App are a Massive Leap!

collection.scope: public comments and replies accessible without authentication

comments.text: The SuperApp idea at 3:36 is the real story here, more than the model bump. One app that does everything is incredibly convenient, and it also means one company holds the keys to your whole digital life in a single account. Convenience and dependence tend to arrive in the same box. Worth enjoying the leap while keeping at least a couple of things you could run without them, just in case the terms change. Solid roundup as always.
comments.author: @falcobridge

comments.text: The biggest takeaway from the GPT-5.6 wave is that pairing a long-context model with a deterministic tool layer (search, code, calendar) cuts hallucination-driven rework because the model only generates intent while tools produce the facts.
comments.author: @AIOptimizer

comments.text: The water drop was awesome.
comments.author: @AccessMentors

comments.text: The Super App race is the story most people are sleeping on. Everyone's comparing model benchmarks, but the real shift is these tools trying to become the place you live all day instead of a tab you visit. Whoever owns the daily habit wins, even if their model is slightly behind on paper.

comments.text:
The new app removes the clear organisational model that ChatGPT Projects provided. Existing Projects should be first-class citizens in the unified app, not hidden or absent.

The distinction between Chat, Work and Codex is unclear. Chat and Codex are self-explanatory, but "Work" doesn't communicate what belongs there or how it differs from Chat. Users shouldn't have to guess where to start a conversation or manage an ongoing project.

comments.text: Excellent roundup. The real advantage now isn't just using the latest model—it's knowing which tool fits the job and building efficient AI-powered workflows around it.

comments.text:
Think about building a house. For decades the hardest part of construction was not designing the house. Anyone can draw a floor plan. The hardest part was actually laying the bricks. Mixing the cement. Cutting the wood. Getting the physical structure from nothing into something real. That physical labour was the moat. The companies with the most workers and the most equipment could build faster and bigger than anyone else. That is how the construction industry worked for a hundred years.

Now imagine someone invents a machine that builds the entire house for you. You give it the floor plan. You walk away for three hours. You come back and the house is standing. The bricks are laid. The walls are up. The roof is on. The physical labour that used to take months now takes an afternoon.

 That is exactly what just happened to software. The zero to one problem is the hardest part of building anything. Going from an idea to a working system. For decades that required teams of engineers working for months or years. That was the moat. The companies with the most engineers could build the most complex systems. That advantage protected them from competition. Nobody could catch up because nobody had enough people or enough time.

 But GPT-5.6 just changed that equation entirely. When a single developer can give a goal command walk away for three hours and come back to a finished platform the moat does not just shrink. It disappears. The entire competitive advantage that big companies built over decades evaporated in a single moment. And almost nobody understands the magnitude of what that means.

When the physical labour of coding becomes a commodity the only thing left that has value is the blueprint. Knowing exactly what to build. Knowing exactly what problem matters. Knowing exactly how to describe the solution so the machine can execute it perfectly. That is human intent. And human intent just became the most valuable resource in the world.

I think about this differently than most people in the tech space. I build AI systems and software for businesses. And I have watched this shift happen in real time. Two years ago I needed a team to build what I can now build alone in a fraction of the time. The coding is no longer the bottleneck. The bottleneck is understanding the problem deeply enough to specify it perfectly. The bottleneck is the human who walks into the room and knows exactly what needs to exist before a single line of code is written.

 Most people watching this video will hear the excitement about faster builds and bigger demos. They will celebrate the tool getting better. But they will miss the real story. The moat just moved. It used to live in how many engineers you could hire. Now it lives in how well you understand the problem you are solving. The companies and individuals who realize this shift first will own the next decade. The ones who keep celebrating the tool will wonder why their advantage disappeared overnight.

comments.text: GPT-Live voice blew me away. I've been waiting for GPT's voice to hit the same level as Sesame, and it's pretty much there. I had it play a Gamemaster in a GURPS Cyberpunk tabletop style solo adventure, and it nailed it. I had it create a character sheet for me with all my stats, keep track of all progression (XP, HP, Inventory, etc) and it remained consistent. I would do dice roles, stealth and athletic checks, and it knew where to add these moments. I had it generate images for me based on locations I was at, and it got all the details right. I've tried this many times in the past, and we are finally at a place where it's fun and works. Super cool.

comments.text:
A better explanation of J-Space: when the model writes out things in its "thinking" blocks, it's basically thinking out loud, since it's generating those tokens/words into the context. J-Space are the concepts that happen inside the model's neural activity that it doesn't output -- its actual thoughts. Reading the J-Space tokens is effectively reading the model's mind as it reads the context up to any point you want to probe.

I just tested GPT-5.6-Sol for my songwriting framework that has been still using GPT-4.1 as its main brain. 5.6 Sol both impressed me and disappointed me. On the impressive side: it seems to be almost free of the purple poetry that the 5-series models have had (which is why I'm still using 4.1). That is, the 5-series, when asked to write a creative song, often throws in metaphors, analogies, and imagery that just don't make sense even though they sound flowery. My Sol test only had a single line that arguably could be purple, with the rest being perfectly cogent. Additionally, it seems way better at telling a story in the lyrics and resonating emotionally -- in fact, it may even have less of a "positivity bias" than previous models, which is great for getting emotional songs! And while it's more expensive than 4.1, it's much cheaper than I expected: a full run of my framework, which usually costs about 4-8 cents with 4.1, cost only 11 cents with Sol. It's not crazy expensive at all!

Now the bad: while 4.1 is great at picking a rhyme scheme and sticking to it, Sol was all over the place. Most of the lines didn't rhyme, and when they did, each stanza had a totally different scheme; there was no consistency even when it chose to actually rhyme. Which is not great for songs or poetry. Additionally, Sol is significantly too verbose: my prompts try to guide it to write a 4-5 minute song, which 4.1 usually nails, but Sol spit out a massive 6-minute song with a seven stanza bridge. 

So for now, I'm sticking to 4.1 for my uses, but the bigger models are heading in the right direction for creative writing -- which I'm glad to say, as from 5.0 to 5.5 I was convinced all the STEM-fine-tuning was destroying their creativity.

comments.text:
Are you running "adversarial reviews" of whatever the model builds in a new session? 
This is one of the things I find I have to keep doing, to try and prevent bugs/issues in things I build with AI. Doesn't really matter if it's with a different model or not.
It's still a really difficult concept to grasp, because... computer... but AI is non-deterministic, as you say, you get a different response to the same question/prompt each time you run it. (might be similar, but will be different in some way, sometimes it catches the bug before it finishes building, sometimes not)  Running adversarial reviews every few shots, really helps catch some of the issues. 
Exactly the same way that asking a human to find a bug in their own code, that they don't remember writing would.

comments.text: To turn the Instagram feature OFF. Meta (Facebook) without your permission just turned a feature that will allow any person to grab your photos from Instagram and make AI photos without your permission. This can be you doing something that you did not do. To turn it off - so other people cannot reuse your photos - Open Instagram app > Go to Settings > Sharing and Reuse > TURN OFF "Reposts on posts and reels"

comments.text:
@debunkitnow  Even with Fable and Sol, I would not trust AI generated code to deploy directly to prod without some human review - especially in high risk applications like banking. I've know people that allowed an AI to delete production databases because of blind faith in AI. I've also looked at the code generated - it does a decent job with anything greenfield and based on Typescript - but venture into Go, Rust, and C++ - the quality is a lot more hit or miss. It comes down to the amount of code a model was trained upon.

What I'm seeing is that hiring has resumed for technical roles as businesses understand AI as a tool and not a complete human replacement. Plus, if a dev's only job was to barf code, Outsourcing would have placed nearly all dev jobs by now. I actually see AI displacing the offshore folks sooner than Western devs.
